items.title: A Social Identity Theory of Digital Identity
items.author: William J. Bingley, Peter Worthy, Janet Wiles, S. Alexander Haslam
items.content: <article>Perspectives on Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. People increasingly live their lives online, which means that their identities are increasingly constituted by and displayed through their activities on digital platforms. items.title: Reframing the Performance and Ethics of Empathic AI: Wisdom of the Crowd and Placebos
items.author: Mark A. ThorntonDepartment of Psychological and Brain Sciences, Dartmouth College
items.content: <article>Perspectives on Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. Recently, claims have emerged that artificial intelligence (AI) is better at providing empathy than humans. These claims are based on experiments in which large language models were prompted to generate empathic responses to short emotional passages. ...</article>

items.title: People’s Responses to Nuclear Weapons: Mapping Post-Cold War Research
items.author: Astrid Kause, Helen Fischer, Zia Mian, Susan T. Fiske
items.content: <article>Perspectives on Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. Nuclear weapon threats are increasing and may be comparable to levels not seen since the worst periods of the Cold War. There could be value in psychologists documenting and explaining people’s responses to nuclear weapons. More than 3 decades have passed ...</article>

items.title: Presence Is Reality: Rethinking Virtual and Real-World Consciousness
items.author: Oliver Singleton, Aikaterini FotopoulouClinical, Educational and Health Psychology Research Department, Division of Psychology and Language Sciences, University College London
items.content: <article>Perspectives on Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. The sense of presence is typically defined as the feeling of “being there” in a virtual environment, whereas the sense of reality is defined as the ability to discriminate between real and unreal phenomena. We challenge this rigid dichotomy, arguing that ...</article>

items.title: Socioeconomic Status Disparities in Children’s Cognition—Differences in Degree or Kind?
items.author: Lingyan Hu, Martha J. Farah
items.content: <article>Perspectives on Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. The “socioeconomic achievement gap” refers to socioeconomic disparities in children’s academic outcomes. Do these gaps invariably reflect cognitive processes that are similar in kind across the socioeconomic status (SES) spectrum but differ quantitatively ...</article>
